source_paper_title: On the Set of Balanced Games

context:
Introduction, page 2 (discussion of minimal balanced collections and enumeration); also referenced indirectly in Section 5.5 via the number of facets depending on the number of minimal balanced collections.

Indeed, the concept of minimal balanced collection generalizes the concept of partitions, and their enumeration remains a problem.
title: Enumerate minimal balanced collections on n players in cooperative games
background_latex:
Let \(N=\{1,\dots,n\}\). A (transferable-utility) cooperative game is a set function \(v:2^N\to\mathbb{R}\) with \(v(\emptyset)=0\).

A family \(\mathcal{B}\subseteq 2^N\setminus\{\emptyset\}\) of nonempty coalitions is called a balanced collection if there exist strictly positive weights \(\lambda_S>0\) for \(S\in\mathcal{B}\) such that each player is covered with total weight 1:
\[
\sum_{S\in\mathcal{B}:\ i\in S} \lambda_S = 1,\qquad \forall i\in N.
\]
A balanced collection \(\mathcal{B}\) is minimal if it contains no proper subcollection that is balanced. (Equivalently, \(\mathcal{B}\) is inclusion-minimal among balanced collections.)

Write \(\mathcal{B}^*(n)\) for the set of all minimal balanced collections on \(N\) excluding the trivial collection \(\{N\}\). It is known that \(\mathcal{B}^*(n)\) is finite and that any minimal balanced collection has cardinality at most \(n\).
problem_definition_latex: Given \(n\ge 1\), determine (exactly) the set \(\mathcal{B}^*(n)\) of all minimal balanced collections on \(N=\{1,\dots,n\}\), or at least determine \(|\mathcal{B}^*(n)|\) (the number of minimal balanced collections) as an explicit function of \(n\).
latex:
Let \(N=\{1,\dots,n\}\). A (transferable-utility) cooperative game is a set function \(v:2^N\to\mathbb{R}\) with \(v(\emptyset)=0\).

A family \(\mathcal{B}\subseteq 2^N\setminus\{\emptyset\}\) of nonempty coalitions is called a balanced collection if there exist strictly positive weights \(\lambda_S>0\) for \(S\in\mathcal{B}\) such that each player is covered with total weight 1:
\[
\sum_{S\in\mathcal{B}:\ i\in S} \lambda_S = 1,\qquad \forall i\in N.
\]
A balanced collection \(\mathcal{B}\) is minimal if it contains no proper subcollection that is balanced. (Equivalently, \(\mathcal{B}\) is inclusion-minimal among balanced collections.)

Write \(\mathcal{B}^*(n)\) for the set of all minimal balanced collections on \(N\) excluding the trivial collection \(\{N\}\). It is known that \(\mathcal{B}^*(n)\) is finite and that any minimal balanced collection has cardinality at most \(n\).

Given \(n\ge 1\), determine (exactly) the set \(\mathcal{B}^*(n)\) of all minimal balanced collections on \(N=\{1,\dots,n\}\), or at least determine \(|\mathcal{B}^*(n)|\) (the number of minimal balanced collections) as an explicit function of \(n\).
location_in_paper: Introduction, page 2 (discussion of minimal balanced collections and enumeration); also referenced indirectly in Section 5.5 via the number of facets depending on the number of minimal balanced collections.
open_problem_quote: Indeed, the concept of minimal balanced collection generalizes the concept of partitions, and their enumeration remains a problem.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: This is an enumeration/characterization problem rather than a universal inequality where one searches for a counterexample. While one can brute-force small \(n\), the search space of set families grows doubly exponentially and does not naturally fit a counterexample-finding workflow.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: An evolutionary coding loop could search for patterns, recurrences, or constructive generation algorithms for minimal balanced collections and validate them computationally for small \(n\). However, scaling and proving correctness for general \(n\) is nontrivial, so automated search is more likely to yield conjectures than full solutions.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The enumeration problem asks for an explicit description of \(\mathcal B^*(n)\), the inclusion-minimal balanced collections (excluding \(\{N\}\)). In the Bondareva--Shapley framework, each \(\mathcal B\in\mathcal B^*(n)\) yields a single linear inequality \(\sum_{S\in\mathcal B}\lambda_S v(S)\le v(N)\) that is necessary and sufficient (over all \(\mathcal B^*(n)\)) for core nonemptiness. The 2025 paper \(\textit{On the set of balanced games}\) leverages this to give a polyhedral description of the cone \(BG(n)\) of balanced games: minimal balanced collections are in bijection with facets of \(BG(n)\) (and similarly for the affine slice \(BG_\alpha(n)\)), so enumerating \(\mathcal B^*(n)\) is equivalent to enumerating all facet-defining inequalities of these polyhedra.

Despite this strong geometric motivation, neither \(\textit{On the set of balanced games}\) nor the earlier focused study \(\textit{Minimal balanced collections and their application to core stability and other topics of game theory}\) provides a closed-form formula for \(|\mathcal B^*(n)|\) or a complete classification of \(\mathcal B^*(n)\) for general \(n\). Existing work instead develops structural properties (e.g., uniqueness of balancing weights for m.b.c., size bound \(|\mathcal B|\le n\), complement operation \(\mathcal B\mapsto \{N\setminus S:S\in\mathcal B\}\), and how tight m.b.c. constraints determine effective coalitions) and polyhedral tools for facet analysis in related cones (e.g., totally balanced games). The state of the art therefore supports algorithmic/structural approaches but leaves the exact enumeration of \(\mathcal B^*(n)\) as an open combinatorial problem.

Promising directions suggested by the polyhedral viewpoint are: (i) treat \(\mathcal B^*(n)\) as the set of circuits/minimal supports of positive solutions to \(A\lambda=\mathbf 1\) where \(A\) is the incidence matrix of coalitions vs. players, linking the problem to oriented matroids and circuit enumeration; (ii) exploit the facet-bijection to compute \(\mathcal B^*(n)\) for moderate \(n\) via polyhedral software and then attempt pattern recognition/recurrences; and (iii) use symmetry under \(S_n\) and the complement operation to reduce the search space and classify m.b.c. up to isomorphism.

sources.title: On the set of balanced games
sources.theorem_or_result: Reformulates balancedness via minimal balanced collections \(\mathcal B\in\mathcal B^*(n)\) and shows these collections are in bijection with facets of the balanced-game cone \(BG(n)\) and of the affine slice \(BG_\alpha(n)\). For the nonnegative normalized polytope \(BG^+(n)\), vertices are exactly balanced \(0\!\)-\(1\)-valued games; vertex enumeration reduces to counting families \(\mathcal D\subseteq 2^N\setminus\{\emptyset,N\}\) with nonempty intersection, yielding a recursion and identification with OEIS A051381.
sources.connection: The open problem asks to enumerate minimal balanced collections \(\mathcal B^*(n)\). This paper emphasizes that \(\mathcal B^*(n)\) is finite, \(|\mathcal B|\le n\), and that each \(\mathcal B\in\mathcal B^*(n)\) corresponds to a facet inequality \(\sum_{S\in\mathcal B}\lambda_S v(S)\le v(N)\). It does not enumerate \(\mathcal B^*(n)\) but provides structural context and shows why enumeration would give an explicit facet description of \(BG(n)\), \(BG_\alpha(n)\), and \(BG^+(n)\).

sources.title: Minimal balanced collections and their application to core stability and other topics of game theory
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops theory of minimal balanced collections (m.b.c.), including their role in describing effective coalitions \(E(v)\) and tight Bondareva--Shapley inequalities; provides structural results and applications to core stability, but does not give a closed-form enumeration of all m.b.c. for general \(n\).
sources.connection: Directly studies minimal balanced collections, the objects to be enumerated in \(\mathcal B^*(n)\), and clarifies how they arise as tight constraints. Likely contains classification results for special families and tools that could support enumeration algorithms.

sources.title: Facets of the cone of totally balanced games
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies facet structure of cones of totally balanced games (and related cones), giving polyhedral/combinatorial methods for identifying facets and extreme rays; connects to balancedness constraints though in a slightly different modeling convention in some literature.
sources.connection: Provides polyhedral techniques and facet-analysis methodology relevant because minimal balanced collections correspond to facets of \(BG(n)\). While focused on totally balanced games, its tools may help algorithmic enumeration or characterization of facet-defining inequalities arising from minimal balanced collections.

sources.title: On balanced sets and cores
sources.bibtex:
@article{Shapley1967BalancedSetsCores,
  title   = {On balanced sets and cores},
  author  = {Shapley, Lloyd S.},
  journal = {Naval Research Logistics Quarterly},
  volume  = {14},
  number  = {4},
  pages   = {453--460},
  year    = {1967},
  doi     = {10.1002/nav.3800140404},
  url     = {https://doi.org/10.1002/nav.3800140404}
}
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces balanced collections and the balancedness condition for nonemptiness of the core; establishes foundational properties of balanced sets/collections used in the Bondareva--Shapley theorem.
sources.connection: Original source of the definition and motivation for minimal balanced collections; provides baseline properties (existence of balancing weights, role in core nonemptiness) that underlie the enumeration problem.

sources.title: Some applications of linear programming to the theory of cooperative games
sources.theorem_or_result: Proves (in Russian) the linear-programming condition equivalent to nonemptiness of the core, later paired with Shapley’s work as the Bondareva--Shapley theorem; introduces balancedness-type constraints.
sources.connection: Foundational motivation: minimal balanced collections are the minimal certificates needed to check balancedness via finitely many inequalities; enumeration would make the theorem computationally explicit.
